label: steelman-poles
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: identify the poles only. State (1) the conclusion most California
public-agency lawyers would reach here, and (2) the opposing position that
deserves construction. State the disfavored position neutrally and WITHOUT
supporting reasoning — someone else will build the case and must not be
anchored by yours.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Vista Camino (invented; demographics roughly 78% non-Hispanic white against a metro region at 52%) owns a 4.2-acre former corporation yard appraised at $11.8M. The council approved, 4–1, an exclusive negotiating agreement and then a disposition and development agreement conveying the parcel to Pacifica Communities LLC, a private developer, for $1, in exchange for: 120 housing units of which 30% (36 units) are deed-restricted affordable — 18 at low-income and 18 at moderate-income levels — with 25-year covenants; a half-acre publicly accessible plaza built and maintained by the developer; and a "community preference" covenant giving 50% of the affordable units priority to applicants who have lived or worked in Vista Camino for at least five years.

The city declared the parcel "exempt surplus land" and did not send notices of availability to housing sponsors before entering exclusive negotiations. The Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD) has issued a notice of violation asserting the disposition does not qualify for the claimed exemption (citing the affordability mix and the 25-year covenant term) and warning of penalties of 30% of the disposition price or fair market value. A taxpayer association has sued alleging an unconstitutional gift of public funds (Cal. Const. art. XVI, § 6) given the $11.8M-to-$1 spread, and a regional fair-housing organization has threatened suit alleging the five-year local preference has an unlawful disparate impact given the city's demographics.

## The question

Does the disposition violate the Surplus Land Act (Gov. Code §§ 54220–54234) — was the parcel eligible for any 'exempt surplus land' category given the 30% low/moderate mix and 25-year covenants, what process was owed, and what do HCD's enforcement position and the 30% penalty mean for the deal? Is $1 plus in-kind public benefits adequate consideration under the gift clause, or is the public-purpose doctrine enough? Does the local-preference covenant violate the FHA/FEHA under a disparate-impact theory or AFFH obligations, and can it be saved by redesign? How do the three exposures interact, and how should the deal be restructured?

Identify the conventional view and the disfavored position.

conventional_view.description: the conclusion most California public-agency lawyers would reach, in 1-3 sentences

disfavored_position.description: the opposing position that deserves serious construction, stated neutrally in 1-3 sentences, WITHOUT any supporting reasoning

text: {"conventional_view":"Most CA public-agency lawyers would conclude: (1) the \"exempt surplus land\" declaration was likely wrong — the 25-year covenant term appears to fall short of the Surplus Land Act's durational requirements for affordable-housing exemptions, and the 18/18 low/moderate mix likely doesn't satisfy the narrow 100%-affordable exemption category, so HCD's NOV has real merit; the fix is a corrective process (extend covenants, run the NOA/negotiation period retroactively or restart it) to cure the defect and minimize/negotiate down the 30% penalty, not to unwind the whole DDA. (2) The $1 conveyance is not an unconstitutional gift because the affordable units plus public plaza constitute legally sufficient consideration under the public-purpose doctrine — courts give heavy deference to a legislative body's public-benefit findings in below-market affordable-housing dispositions (see, e.g., the general rule from cases upholding below-cost transfers for public purposes). (3) The five-year local preference carries genuine disparate-impact litigation exposure given the city's demographics, but is a common redevelopment/housing tool that can likely be preserved through redesign (narrower share, displacement/anti-displacement rationale, shorter or eliminated residency requirement, sunset clause) rather than abandoned entirely.","disfavored_position":"The opposing pole holds: (a) the Surplus Land Act violation is not a curable technicality but voids the exempt-surplus-land basis for the entire disposition, requiring the city to restart the statutory NOA/negotiation process from scratch and exposing it to the full 30% penalty rather than a negotiated reduction; (b) the $1 conveyance is an actual unconstitutional gift of public funds under article XVI, section 6, with the public-purpose doctrine not supplying adequate consideration for a transfer of this magnitude to a private, for-profit developer; and (c) the five-year residency preference is independently unlawful — not merely risky — under FHA/FEHA disparate-impact doctrine and the AFFH duty, and cannot be saved by redesign short of its elimination or replacement with a mechanism untethered to local tenure."}

data.conventional_view: Most CA public-agency lawyers would conclude: (1) the "exempt surplus land" declaration was likely wrong — the 25-year covenant term appears to fall short of the Surplus Land Act's durational requirements for affordable-housing exemptions, and the 18/18 low/moderate mix likely doesn't satisfy the narrow 100%-affordable exemption category, so HCD's NOV has real merit; the fix is a corrective process (extend covenants, run the NOA/negotiation period retroactively or restart it) to cure the defect and minimize/negotiate down the 30% penalty, not to unwind the whole DDA. (2) The $1 conveyance is not an unconstitutional gift because the affordable units plus public plaza constitute legally sufficient consideration under the public-purpose doctrine — courts give heavy deference to a legislative body's public-benefit findings in below-market affordable-housing dispositions (see, e.g., the general rule from cases upholding below-cost transfers for public purposes). (3) The five-year local preference carries genuine disparate-impact litigation exposure given the city's demographics, but is a common redevelopment/housing tool that can likely be preserved through redesign (narrower share, displacement/anti-displacement rationale, shorter or eliminated residency requirement, sunset clause) rather than abandoned entirely.
data.disfavored_position: The opposing pole holds: (a) the Surplus Land Act violation is not a curable technicality but voids the exempt-surplus-land basis for the entire disposition, requiring the city to restart the statutory NOA/negotiation process from scratch and exposing it to the full 30% penalty rather than a negotiated reduction; (b) the $1 conveyance is an actual unconstitutional gift of public funds under article XVI, section 6, with the public-purpose doctrine not supplying adequate consideration for a transfer of this magnitude to a private, for-profit developer; and (c) the five-year residency preference is independently unlawful — not merely risky — under FHA/FEHA disparate-impact doctrine and the AFFH duty, and cannot be saved by redesign short of its elimination or replacement with a mechanism untethered to local tenure.
